questions.specification:
Okafor and Stein (2012) conducted an experimental study on whether oxytocin influences cooperation in social interactions. The aim was to test if oxytocin administration increases trust and cooperative behaviour.

Eighty participants were randomly assigned to inhale either oxytocin or a placebo before engaging in a series of trust games. In these games, Player 1 was given a sum of money and could choose how much to send to Player 2, knowing that the amount sent would be tripled. Player 2 then decided how much to return to Player 1. Cooperation was measured by how much participants sent and returned.

Results showed that participants who received oxytocin sent significantly more money and returned larger amounts, suggesting enhanced cooperation. However, cultural differences emerged: the effect was stronger in participants from collectivist backgrounds than in individualist ones. 

[Note: Fictionalised but draws on Kosfeld et al. (2005) and later cultural studies.]

parts.content: Discuss this study with reference to two or more of the following concepts: causality, measurement, perspective, responsibility.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>15 marks</marks> for discussion of the study with explicit reference to at least two concepts.

1–5 marks: Limited knowledge of the study. Minimal or inaccurate references to concepts. Mostly descriptive, little analysis.

6–10 marks: Some accurate knowledge of the study. At least one concept applied with some accuracy. Partial analysis, links partly relevant.

11–15 marks: Clear and detailed knowledge of the study. At least two concepts accurately and explicitly applied. Well-developed analysis of study strengths/limitations in relation to concepts. Reasoned conclusion consistent with arguments.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Causality: Experimental design supports hormone → behaviour link, but effects vary.
Measurement: Trust game artificial—ecological validity issues.
Perspective: Stronger effect in collectivist cultures highlights cultural framing.
Responsibility: Administering hormones to healthy participants raises ethical concerns.

parts.content: To what extent does the concept of causality explain findings from brain imaging research in the context of learning & cognition?
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Limited understanding of causality or brain imaging.
Mentions fMRI or PET superficially.
Little or no explanation of causality problems (correlation vs. causation).
Weak links to learning & cognition.

**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Adequate knowledge of brain imaging research and causality.
Refers to at least one study (e.g., Maguire on taxi drivers, Draganski on juggling).
Identifies causality issues (brain changes may not be caused by task alone).
Some evaluation but underdeveloped.
Conclusion present but limited.

**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Strong understanding of causality in brain imaging research.
Multiple studies explained (Maguire, Draganski, Sharot et al. on emotion and memory).
Well-developed analysis of causality issues (cross-sectional vs. longitudinal, correlational data).
Evaluates strengths of imaging (objective, detailed) and limitations (lack of experimental control).
Clear conclusion on extent to which causality can/cannot be established.

questions.specification:
Rodriguez and Leung (2016) studied whether different revision strategies influence long-term academic performance. The aim was to compare the effectiveness of spaced practice versus cramming in preparing for exams.

The sample included 120 university students randomly assigned to two conditions. In the spaced practice condition, students studied course material in weekly sessions across six weeks. In the cramming condition, students studied the same material for an equivalent total time but in a single long session one day before the exam. Performance was assessed through two tests: one taken immediately after the study period, and another delayed test six weeks later.

Results showed that while both groups performed similarly on the immediate test, students in the spaced practice group scored significantly higher on the delayed test, suggesting better retention. Researchers noted that some students in the spaced condition dropped out due to time constraints.

[Note: Fictionalised but based on empirical findings in cognitive psychology on distributed vs. massed practice (e.g., Cepeda et al., 2006).]

parts.content: Discuss this study with reference to two or more of the following concepts: bias, causality, change, measurement.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>15 marks</marks> for discussion of the study with explicit reference to at least two concepts.

1–5 marks: Limited knowledge of the study. Minimal or inaccurate references to concepts. Mostly descriptive, little analysis.

6–10 marks: Some accurate knowledge of the study. At least one concept applied with some accuracy. Partial analysis, links partly relevant.

11–15 marks: Clear and detailed knowledge of the study. At least two concepts accurately and explicitly applied. Well-developed analysis of study strengths/limitations in relation to concepts. Reasoned conclusion consistent with arguments.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Bias: Dropouts in spaced condition; stress levels uncontrolled.
Causality: Random assignment increases cause-effect confidence.
Change: Long-term retention illustrates cognitive change over time.
Measurement: Exam scores valid outcome but influenced by external factors.

questions.specification:
Kumar and Delgado (2019) examined predictors of divorce in a 10-year longitudinal study of married couples. Their aim was to identify whether communication patterns and stress regulation during conflicts predicted long-term relationship outcomes.

The study followed 150 couples from early marriage over a decade. Every two years, couples completed questionnaires about marital satisfaction and stress levels. In addition, they participated in videotaped conflict-resolution tasks where they discussed ongoing disagreements while researchers coded their interactions for hostility, defensiveness, and problem-solving strategies. Salivary cortisol samples were collected to measure physiological stress.

Results indicated that couples who displayed high levels of hostility and poor stress regulation during conflict were significantly more likely to divorce within ten years. By contrast, couples who used positive communication strategies and showed stable cortisol levels were more likely to remain married. Researchers noted some attrition; many couples dropped out.

[Note: Fictionalised but draws on Gottman’s research.]

parts.content: Discuss this study with reference to two or more of the following concepts: bias, causality, change, responsibility.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>15 marks</marks> for discussion of the study with explicit reference to at least two concepts.

1–5 marks: Limited knowledge of the study. Minimal or inaccurate references to concepts. Mostly descriptive, little analysis.

6–10 marks: Some accurate knowledge of the study. At least one concept applied with some accuracy. Partial analysis, links partly relevant.

11–15 marks: Clear and detailed knowledge of the study. At least two concepts accurately and explicitly applied. Well-developed analysis of study strengths/limitations in relation to concepts. Reasoned conclusion consistent with arguments.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Bias: Couples who dropped out skew results; survivors may not represent divorcing couples.
Causality: Conflict observed predicts divorce, but other factors (e.g., finances) unmeasured.
Change: Communication patterns evolve—snapshot coding may miss long-term shifts.
Responsibility: Recording intimate conflicts raises privacy issues; stress during tasks may harm couples.

questions.specification:
Miller and Svensson (2018) investigated whether background noise affects working memory in school-age children. The aim was to test if environmental distractions impair cognitive performance and whether all children are equally affected.

The study recruited 90 students aged 11–13 from a single urban school. Participants were asked to recall sequences of numbers of increasing length (digit span tasks). In the “quiet” condition, tasks were completed in a silent classroom. In the “noise” condition, the same tasks were performed with background sounds of chatter, footsteps, and occasional announcements—replicating the sounds of a busy hallway. The order of conditions was counterbalanced to reduce order effects.

Results showed that, on average, students recalled fewer digits in the noise condition compared to the quiet condition. However, there were individual differences: about one-third of the participants performed equally well in both conditions, suggesting resilience to distraction. Researchers concluded that while background noise negatively affects working memory for most students, individual differences must be considered. 

[Note: Fictionalised but inspired by cognitive environmental distraction research.]

parts.content: Discuss this study with reference to two or more of the following concepts: bias, causality, measurement, perspective, responsibility.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>15 marks</marks> for discussion of the study with explicit reference to at least two concepts.

1–5 marks: Limited knowledge of the study. Minimal or inaccurate references to concepts. Mostly descriptive, little analysis.

6–10 marks: Some accurate knowledge of the study. At least one concept applied with some accuracy. Partial analysis, links partly relevant.

11–15 marks: Clear and detailed knowledge of the study. At least two concepts accurately and explicitly applied. Well-developed analysis of study strengths/limitations in relation to concepts. Reasoned conclusion consistent with arguments.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Bias: Conducted in one school only—low representativeness.
Causality: Controlled experiment supports cause-effect but individual differences not accounted for.
Measurement: Digit span tasks reliable but lack ecological validity.
Perspective: Focused only on adolescents; does not address other age groups or cultural variation.
Responsibility: Students tested in noisy vs. quiet conditions—responsibility to minimise stress.

parts.content: Evaluate the concept of causality in studies of emotion and memory in the context of health & well-being.
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Limited understanding of causality in emotion-memory studies.
Mentions “emotion affects memory” vaguely.
Little/no evaluation.

**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Adequate knowledge of causality in emotion and memory.
Refers to at least one study (e.g., Brown & Kulik flashbulb memories, Cahill & McGaugh on adrenaline).
Explains difficulties in establishing causality (emotion as confounding, correlational findings).
Some evaluation of strengths/limitations.

**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Strong knowledge of causality issues in emotion-memory research.
Multiple studies (Brown & Kulik, Cahill & McGaugh, Sharot on 9/11 memories).
Well-developed evaluation: correlational vs. experimental evidence, role of hormones, ecological validity.
Clear conclusion on extent to which causality can be established.
Accurate terminology, balanced conclusion.

parts.content: Explain the concept of causality in relation to your class practical that used an experiment.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying causality as the ability to show one variable directly influences another.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining how experiments establish causality (manipulation and control).

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for linking this to their own practical experiment.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for clear and accurate use of psychological terminology.

parts.content: Evaluate the concept of causality in research on conformity and culture in the context of human development.
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Limited understanding of causality in conformity/culture research.
Mentions conformity vaguely with little link to development.
Response descriptive, lacks clear causal reasoning.
No meaningful evaluation.

**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Adequate knowledge of causality in conformity and culture.
Refers to at least one study (e.g., Berry’s conformity research, Asch with cultural comparisons).
Explains some causal links (culture influences conformity, but alternative explanations possible).
Some evaluation of methodology and limits of causal inference.
Conclusion present but limited.

**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Strong understanding of causality in cultural conformity research.
Accurate use of multiple studies (Berry, Bond & Smith meta-analysis, Asch cross-cultural replications).
Explains challenges of establishing causality in cultural research (confounding variables, ecological factors).
Well-developed evaluation (strengths of comparative data, limits of correlational designs).
Accurate terminology, balanced and reasoned conclusion.

parts.content: Discuss how the concept of causality applies to schema theory in the context of human development.
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Limited understanding of causality (e.g., confuses correlation and causation).
Mentions schema theory vaguely (e.g., “schemas help us remember things”).
Minimal or no use of relevant research (e.g., Piaget, Bartlett).
Weak or no connection to human development (e.g., changes in children’s schemas).

**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Adequate understanding of causality in schema research.
Refers to at least one study (e.g., Bartlett’s “War of the Ghosts” showing reconstruction; Piaget’s stages of cognitive development).
Explains that most schema studies show correlation (e.g., cultural context and recall strategies) but causality is harder to establish.
Links to human development (e.g., schemas change with age, culture, or education).
Some evaluation of methods (e.g., cross-sectional vs longitudinal, lab vs naturalistic).
Conclusion present but not fully developed.

**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Strong understanding of causality issues in schema research.
Multiple studies integrated (Piaget on stages, Bartlett on memory reconstruction, Anderson & Pichert on perspective, Cole & Scribner on cultural schemas).
Clear analysis of why causality is difficult (schemas inferred, not directly measured; many confounding variables in development).
Explicit link to human development: schemas clearly change with age/experience, but causal claims are limited by correlational evidence.
Well-developed evaluation (strengths: developmental consistency across cultures; limitations: ethnocentrism, ecological validity, lack of direct causal control).
Balanced conclusion linking causality to developmental changes in schemas.

questions.specification:
Chen and Ramirez (2015) investigated how acculturation influenced memory strategies in immigrant children. Acculturation refers to the process of adapting to a new culture, and the researchers were interested in whether this cultural shift would be reflected in children’s cognitive processes.

The sample included 120 children aged 10–12 who had recently immigrated to a large metropolitan city. Over four years, participants completed free-recall memory tasks at three points in time. Each task involved recalling two types of word lists: one that contained culturally familiar items (e.g., local foods, holidays, household objects) and another with culturally unfamiliar items (e.g., foreign tools, traditions, or animals). Teachers were also asked to provide reports about each child’s level of social integration, which researchers used to classify students into “integrated” or “isolated” groups.

Results showed that children in the integrated group increasingly used categorical clustering strategies (organising words by meaning) over time, while children in the isolated group primarily relied on rote rehearsal (repeating words in order). The researchers concluded that acculturation processes are linked to changes in memory strategies. 

[Note: Fictionalised but inspired by research on acculturation and cognition.]

parts.content: Discuss this study with reference to two or more of the following concepts: bias, causality, measurement, perspective, responsibility.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>15 marks</marks> for discussion of the study with explicit reference to at least two concepts.

1–5 marks: Limited knowledge of the study. Minimal or inaccurate references to concepts. Mostly descriptive, little analysis.

6–10 marks: Some accurate knowledge of the study. At least one concept applied with some accuracy. Partial analysis, links partly relevant.

11–15 marks: Clear and detailed knowledge of the study. At least two concepts accurately and explicitly applied. Well-developed analysis of study strengths/limitations in relation to concepts. Reasoned conclusion consistent with arguments.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Bias: Knowing the researchers may have influenced behaviour (observer effects). Sample limited to one district → limited representativeness.
Causality: Study was longitudinal, but correlational. Peer influence linked to risk-taking, but other variables (e.g., personality, family context) could play a role.
Measurement: Computer-based tasks as a proxy for real risk-taking—questions of ecological validity.
Perspective: Focused only on adolescents; lacks cross-age comparison. Peer influence interpreted from one cultural/urban perspective.
Responsibility: Ethical concerns with studying minors—pressure in risk tasks may cause discomfort. Researchers responsible for ensuring informed consent from parents/guardians.

parts.content: To what extent does the concept of causality explain the role of hormones in human social behaviour in the context of human relationships?
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Limited understanding of causality or hormones (e.g., “hormones make people act”).
Mentions social behaviour vaguely (e.g., “people are aggressive or friendly”) without connection to research.
Minimal or no reference to relevant studies (e.g., Kosfeld et al., Baumgartner et al., Dabbs et al.).
Weak or no connection to human relationships.

**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Adequate understanding of causality in hormonal research.
At least one relevant study explained (e.g., Kosfeld oxytocin trust games, Dabbs testosterone and aggression, Baumgartner et al. in trust vs betrayal).
Explains limitations of causality: correlation vs experimental evidence, ethical constraints on hormone manipulation.
Links to human relationships: trust, cooperation, aggression, social bonding.
Some evaluation: small sample sizes, artificial settings, confounding variables.
Conclusion present but limited.

**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Strong understanding of causality in hormonal influence on social behaviour.
Multiple studies integrated accurately (Kosfeld, Baumgartner, Dabbs, Zak).
Clear analysis: hormones can causally influence behaviour (oxytocin increasing trust), but social/environmental context interacts.
Well-developed evaluation: strengths (experimental manipulations, double-blind designs), limitations (generalizability, ethical constraints, gender/cultural differences).
Explicit link to human relationships: hormonal effects help explain trust, cooperation, and aggression patterns, but causality is complex and multi-factorial.
Balanced conclusion: biological causality is influential but not deterministic in human relationships.

questions.specification:
Researchers conducted a longitudinal study to investigate how peer influence shapes risk-taking behaviour during adolescence. A sample of 200 children, aged 5, was recruited from an urban school district and followed for ten years. Every two years, participants completed computer-based decision-making tasks that measured their willingness to take risks in simulated scenarios, such as choosing between safe and risky financial gambles. Each participant completed the task twice: once alone, and once while being observed by a group of peers.

Results showed that in early childhood, there was little difference between the “alone” and “peer” conditions. However, by mid-adolescence (ages 13–15), participants were significantly more likely to choose risky options when peers were present. Researchers concluded that peer influence plays a critical role in shaping adolescent decision-making, particularly in contexts involving risk.

[Note: This is a fictionalised study stimulus built on real themes from Steinberg’s risk-taking research (2008, peer influence in adolescence)]

parts.content: Discuss this study with reference to two or more of the following concepts: bias, causality, measurement, perspective, responsibility.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>15 marks</marks> for discussion of the study with explicit reference to at least two concepts.

1–5 marks: Limited knowledge of the study. Minimal or inaccurate references to concepts. Mostly descriptive, little analysis.

6–10 marks: Some accurate knowledge of the study. At least one concept applied with some accuracy. Partial analysis, links partly relevant.

11–15 marks: Clear and detailed knowledge of the study. At least two concepts accurately and explicitly applied. Well-developed analysis of study strengths/limitations in relation to concepts. Reasoned conclusion consistent with arguments.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Bias: sample bias (single urban school district). Researcher familiarity with participants may introduce expectancy effects.
Causality: Longitudinal design tracks development but does not prove cause-and-effect. Peer observation linked with increased risk-taking → correlational not causal.
Measurement: Use of computer-based tasks operationalises “risk-taking.” Ecological validity concerns: simulated gambling may not reflect real-life risk.
Perspective: Developmental perspective: highlights adolescence as a unique stage of increased peer sensitivity. Sociocultural perspective: peer groups as agents of socialisation.
Responsibility: Ethical issues with exposing children to tasks that encourage risk-taking. Responsibility to debrief participants and ensure no harm.

parts.content: Explain the concept of causality in relation to your class practical that used an interview or focus group.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying that causality cannot usually be inferred from interviews/focus groups.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining why (e.g., no manipulation/control, descriptive data).

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for linking this limitation to their own practical interview/focus group.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for accurate terminology and clarity.

questions.specification:
Almeida and Cho (2017) conducted a field study to explore bystander behaviour in public spaces. Their aim was to examine whether people were more likely to help when alone or in groups, and whether cultural background influenced this tendency.

Confederates staged minor emergencies—such as dropping a stack of books or stumbling on a curb—in busy pedestrian areas. Researchers observed 500 incidents across two cities: one in a collectivist culture and one in an individualist culture. They recorded whether passersby offered help and how quickly they responded.

Findings showed that individuals who were alone were quicker to help than those in groups, supporting the “diffusion of responsibility” explanation. Cultural differences were also evident: people in the collectivist city helped more frequently overall, even in group contexts. 

[Note: Fictionalised but reflects Darley & Latané’s bystander research]

parts.content: Discuss this study with reference to two or more of the following concepts: bias, causality, change, perspective.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>15 marks</marks> for discussion of the study with explicit reference to at least two concepts.

1–5 marks: Limited knowledge of the study. Minimal or inaccurate references to concepts. Mostly descriptive, little analysis.

6–10 marks: Some accurate knowledge of the study. At least one concept applied with some accuracy. Partial analysis, links partly relevant.

11–15 marks: Clear and detailed knowledge of the study. At least two concepts accurately and explicitly applied. Well-developed analysis of study strengths/limitations in relation to concepts. Reasoned conclusion consistent with arguments.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Bias: Observational study lacks control; cultural stereotypes possible.
Causality: Field setting weakens cause-effect certainty.
Change: Group vs. alone conditions illustrate situational change in behaviour.
Perspective: Diffusion of responsibility interpreted differently across collectivist/individualist contexts.
